story:
Tires hum. Handcuffs bite. I’m chained in a prison transport, counting miles to the spike strip Terry promised to drop. I’ve been awake for days for this moment. Ten million in diamonds sit where I hid them—the only clean door out of a dirty life, if I can get there. I mouth off at the guards, think about how crooked they were inside—bribed, brutal, blind. I’ve never hurt the weak. I tell myself that counts.

The rig hits the spikes and bucks. Metal screams. We roll. The ceiling kisses my skull. When the world stops, I’m upside down, bleeding but not broken. Shots crack outside. I crawl toward light and cold air. Trees crowd the road. Freedom smells like wet leaves. Terry looms there—huge, bearded, flashlight in my eyes. Behind him, guards lie still. My gut twists. “Did you have to?” He grunts, “Hurry,” and strips a key from a belt to pop my cuffs.

We stumble to his stashed car. From the trunk Terry yanks out the brown bag he’d hidden for me earlier that day: clothes, fake ID, wallet, a wad of cash, knife, handgun. I shed the jumpsuit and kick it into a ditch, scrub my face clean, shove down the shaking. “Start it,” I tell him. The getaway car coughs, then dies. We push it off the road into the trees and take off on foot—ten miles to a station, a train, the city.

The night is sharp, the moon full. A chopper murmurs far off, terror nipping at my heels. We plunge deeper under the canopy. Terry panics, bulls through branches, loses all sense of direction. I try to reel him in. The ground slips away beneath us. He drops without a sound. I call his name. The earth opens under me too.

I wake in a damp cave. Drips tick. Roots net the hole above. No pain, no fresh wounds—like I was placed there. Something watches. I grip the gun. A voice answers the name I shout—not Terry. It’s deep, everywhere. A shape resolves at the edge of the light—pale, huge, draped in shadows, a face oddly calm. He lowers into the faint beam and chews on a human leg. Terry’s. My stomach flips.

I empty the gun. Bullets patter his robes like rain. He barely winces. “Stings,” he says. “I should kill you.” I clutch the knife, tears pricking anyway. “What are you?” He almost smiles. His words crackle with old sorrow: once, his kind walked as kings and gods; human numbers and greed swelled and toppled them. He raises one finger and the air crushes me without touching me. He rips me open—not my body, my self—and reads it: the theft, the violence, the shame I bury, the honest part—freedom, not just money.

He lets me breathe again. “Don’t touch the gun,” he thunders, and I don’t. “You have a heart. That’s all. Few do.” He lifts a weary hand. “You have my watch—my protection—until dawn. You’ll reach what you truly seek. Then you’re on your own.” Darkness folds me like a blanket.

I come to under the moon, above ground. No wounds. No weapons. Cash and ID still in my pocket. The woods open onto a small station. No workers. A kiosk glows. I move like no one sees me. I buy a ticket to the city and board the train. The diamonds are hours away. I feel light, spared, and more alone than ever. Out of the woods. Freedom close. Judgment closer.
